front: What is a subgame in an extensive form game?
back: A subgame is a part of the game that begins at a specific decision node (which is a singleton information set) and includes all subsequent decision nodes and terminal nodes that follow from it. It must form a game itself, adhering to the rules of the original game.

front: What is a Sub-game Perfect Equilibrium (SPE)?
back: A strategy profile is a Sub-game Perfect Equilibrium if it constitutes a Nash Equilibrium in every subgame of the original game. It eliminates non-credible threats by requiring players' strategies to be optimal at every point in the game tree.

front: Describe the Backward Induction algorithm for finding SPE in finite horizon games.
back: Backward induction is the primary method for finding SPE. It starts at the final decision nodes (leaves of the game tree) and works backwards to the root. At each node, the algorithm determines the optimal action for the player who moves there, assuming optimal play will occur in all subsequent subgames. The resulting strategy profile is an SPE.

front: Why does every finite game of perfect information have at least one pure-strategy SPE?
back: Because the backward induction algorithm can be applied. At each final decision node, a player has a well-defined optimal action (which is pure since it's a best response to the fixed future). Moving backwards, each step again involves choosing a pure best response, guaranteeing the existence of at least one pure-strategy equilibrium.

front: What is a key limitation of Nash Equilibrium that Sub-game Perfect Equilibrium addresses?
back: Nash Equilibrium can include non-credible threats—promises or threats a player would not rationally carry out if actually reached. SPE refines the solution concept by requiring the strategy to be optimal off the equilibrium path, making any threats within the equilibrium credible.
